figures.gloss: A Song dynasty shamaness named Lin Moniang who died saving her brothers at sea and was apotheosized into the highest celestial ranks. She is the supreme protector of seafarers and the global Chinese diaspora, answering the direct cries of those in peril.
figures.facet: Her authority stems not from bureaucratic decree but from spontaneous popular devotion and miraculous efficacy. She forced the imperial state to posthumously promote her through the celestial bureaucracy merely to legitimize her unstoppable cult.
figures.provenance: Song dynasty local gazetteers; imperial promotion decrees of the Ming and Qing dynasties; widespread coastal temple inscriptions.

figures.gloss: The apotheosized Han dynasty general Guan Yu, revered as the embodiment of martial valor, unyielding loyalty, and righteousness. As an enforcer of moral oaths, he serves as the patron deity of soldiers, merchants, secret societies, and police forces alike.
figures.facet: He is a god of war who abhors bloodshed. His martial power is entirely subordinate to moral rectitude; he wields his halberd not to conquer, but to exorcise demonic forces and guarantee trust in commercial and fraternal contracts.
figures.provenance: Records of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguozhi); Ming dynasty imperial decrees elevating him to 'Emperor' (Di); vernacular novels.

figures.gloss: The lowest-ranking but most ubiquitous official in the celestial bureaucracy, governing a specific village, neighborhood, or graveyard. Portrayed as a benevolent, white-bearded elder, he keeps the local census, protects crops, and mediates between the human community and the spirit world.
figures.facet: He is intimately vulnerable to human displeasure. If a village suffers famine or misfortune, locals might scold, demote, or physically leave his statue out in the sun until he performs his administrative duties properly.
figures.provenance: Ubiquitous village earth shrines (Tudimiao); Ming and Qing folk tales; local oral traditions.

figures.gloss: The domestic spy of the celestial court, enshrined directly above the family stove. He silently observes the moral conduct of the household, ascending to heaven on the eve of the Lunar New Year to deliver his annual report on the family's virtues and sins.
figures.facet: He bridges state bureaucracy and domestic intimacy. Though an agent of divine surveillance, he is routinely bribed with sticky sweets before his ascent to literally glue his mouth shut, ensuring he only reports favorable things to Heaven.
figures.provenance: Han dynasty texts on domestic sacrifices; traditional folk tales; ubiquitous paper effigies.

figures.gloss: The venerated spirits of a lineage's forebears, enshrined on domestic altars and in clan halls. Fed by ongoing filial sacrifices, they actively intercede to bless their descendants with wealth, male heirs, and academic success while warding off external misfortunes.
figures.facet: The dead are not truly gone, nor are they self-sufficient; they require constant material support from the living. A neglected ancestor becomes a hungry ghost (egui), mutating from a familial protector into a dangerous, wandering parasite.
figures.provenance: The Book of Rites (Liji); lineage genealogies (zupu); ubiquitous domestic altar practices.

figures.gloss: The celestial counterpart to the earthly district magistrate, presiding over the spiritual administration of a walled city. He commands a terrifying retinue of lictors to judge the dead, punish local evils, and protect the urban populace from plagues and droughts.
figures.facet: He forms a perfect mirror of the imperial state. Earthly magistrates explicitly prayed to him, acknowledging a dual governance where the human official ruled the visible realm (yang) while the City God policed the invisible (yin).
figures.provenance: Ming dynasty state cult statutes; city god temple (Chenghuangmiao) stele inscriptions; popular morality books (shanshu).

figures.gloss: The dynamic, miraculous efficacy or 'spiritual power' that animates gods, amulets, and shrines. It is not an innate, permanent divine right, but a transactional energy proven through answered prayers, healed diseases, and averted disasters in the human realm.
figures.facet: Divinity is explicitly meritocratic and transactional. A god with strong 'ling' attracts massive patronage and state promotion; a god who fails to produce 'ling' is abandoned, their temple left to ruin, replaced by a more effective deity.
figures.provenance: Anthropological studies of popular religion; centuries of temple dedication steles recording answered prayers.

edges.note: Local earth gods report directly to the City God, forming the base of the celestial administrative hierarchy.
Both inhabit the domestic sphere and receive household offerings, but one represents kin solidarity while the other represents celestial surveillance.
GOLD SEAM: Mazu is widely syncretized with Guanyin (Avalokitesvara) in coastal regions, viewed either as her incarnation or a complementary goddess of compassion.
GOLD SEAM: A maternal savior goddess who transcends local cult status to become a universal protector of seafarers and merchants across a vast diaspora.
GOLD SEAM: The deified essence of the ocean's power, petitioned directly by coastal communities for safe passage and bountiful catches.
GOLD SEAM: A martial figure wielding divine weapons who is less a god of war than a guardian of oaths, truth, and contracts.
GOLD SEAM: A god of martial prowess whose primary domain is actually justice, covenants, and the lawful ordering of society.
GOLD SEAM: A patron of iron, weaponry, and brotherhoods, revered by both outlaws and the police who hunt them.
GOLD SEAM: The domestic fire revered as a pure, observing presence that acts as a messenger between the human household and supreme celestial justice.
GOLD SEAM: Ancestral spirits who remain intimately involved in family affairs, requiring regular propitiation to prevent their protective presence from becoming destructive.
GOLD SEAM: The collective venerated dead who anchor the lineage, functioning as the primary mediators of blessing and social cohesion for the living.
GOLD SEAM: The forebears whose continued existence depends on ritual remembrance, and who act as strict moral guardians of their descendants' behavior.
GOLD SEAM: The City God's judicial courts in the underworld heavily absorbed the iconography and bureaucratic torture chambers of the Buddhist Yama.
GOLD SEAM: The divine magistrate who oversees the accurate weighing of mortal deeds and the rigorous processing of souls in the afterlife.
GOLD SEAM: An invisible, circulating spiritual currency that empowers beings and objects, expanding through success and dissipating through failure.
GOLD SEAM: The vital, transactional favor of heaven that legitimizes authority, but which can be withdrawn if the mandate is not properly exercised.
GOLD SEAM: A transactional, physical manifestation of divine efficacy that must be actively generated and proven, not just believed in.

GOLD SEAM: Two women deified from below into supreme popular goddesses of their nations, both forcing state ratification of their cults; in southern Vietnam, Mazu (Thiên Hậu) worship interweaves with the Mother cult it parallels.
GOLD SEAM: Both project the imperial bureaucracy into the spirit world — ranked celestial offices mirroring the earthly court — though the Vietnamese version answers to Mothers.

dossier.essence: The diffuse communal religion of Han Chinese populations in mainland China, Taiwan, and the diaspora: worship of gods (shen) arrayed as a celestial bureaucracy under the Jade Emperor, veneration of family ancestors, and propitiation of ghosts. It has no founder, membership, or church — it lives in household, lineage, village temple, and festival — and by participation it is among the largest bodies of religious practice on earth, revived vigorously on the mainland since the 1980s.
dossier.cosmos: The spirit world mirrors imperial society, classically schematized by Arthur Wolf as gods, ghosts, and ancestors: gods are the celestial officialdom (City Gods, Earth Gods, the Kitchen God filing his New Year report), ancestors are one's own senior kin, and ghosts are the neglected or unrelated dead — dangerous strangers. The dead pass through the underworld courts of the Ten Kings (a Buddhist–Daoist synthesis) and depend on descendants' offerings of food and spirit money. Divine status is meritocratic and provisional: a god's efficacy (ling) is proven by answered prayers and forfeited by failure, the path by which deified humans such as Mazu and Guan Yu rose through the ranks.
dossier.practice: Daily incense at household altars and ancestral tablets; temple offerings and vows, with divination by moon-blocks (jiaobei) and numbered lot-sticks; a festival cycle including New Year, Qingming grave-sweeping, and the seventh-month Ghost Festival; processions and pilgrimages on gods' birthdays, the Dajia Mazu pilgrimage in Taiwan among the largest. Spirit mediums (tang-ki) speak for gods in trance in Hokkien and other regional communities. It keeps no clergy of its own, hiring Daoist priests or Buddhist monks for rites as needed; what it asks is reciprocity — keep the offerings, debts, and reports current.
dossier.canon: No canon: an oral-ritual repertoire carried by temple custom, morality books (shanshu), opera, and vernacular novels — 'Journey to the West' and the 'Fengshen Yanyi' (Investiture of the Gods) supplied much of the popular pantheon's imagery.
dossier.reflects: Divinity modeled on government and audited by results — the transactional, civic face of the religious impulse, where worship is a working relationship rather than a creed.
sources: Arthur P. Wolf (ed.), 'Religion and Ritual in Chinese Society' (1974)
C. K. Yang, 'Religion in Chinese Society' (1961)
Adam Yuet Chau, 'Miraculous Response: Doing Popular Religion in Contemporary China' (2006)
Valerie Hansen, 'Changing Gods in Medieval China, 1127–1276' (1990)
